data.title: Gold Hits New High; Dow Rises — WSJ
data.description:
By David Uberti

Gold futures are on track to eclipse their inflation-adjusted record set 45 years ago after it emerged the U.S. had imposed levies on Swiss gold bars.

The move is a fresh blow for Switzerland, a major refining hub, which this week failed to avert broader 39% levies on exports to the U.S.

Gold prices have risen for five of the past six days, boosted in part by hopes the Federal Reserve will soon cut interest rates in response to weakening economic data. Futures prices in New York neared $3,500 a troy ounce Friday.

U.S stocks edged higher Friday and are on track for gains at the end of a week heavy on tariff updates but light on market turbulence. The Dow industrials are currently up more than 1% for the week. The Nasdaq Composite, which hit a record high Thursday, has gained more than 3%.

Late Thursday, President Trump chose economic adviser Stephen Miran to fill a vacancy on the Federal Reserve's board of governors until January. Miran will replace Adriana Kugler, who is expected to depart Friday.

In recent trading:

Stocks indexes crept higher, led by the tech-heavy Nasdaq.

Intel shares wavered after Thursday's steep loss. The chipmaker said CEO Lip-Bu Tan had the board's support, after Trump called for his resignation.

Treasury prices inched lower, pushing up yields. The 10-year yield rose to around 4.28%.

Oil prices are on track for their third weekly loss in the past four weeks. Traders are weighing the impact of OPEC+ supply bumps against potential U.S. sanctions that could take Russian crude off the global market.

Write to David Uberti at david.uberti@wsj.com

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Block Pares Gains as Solid Earnings Fail to Wow Investors — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

Shares of Block, the parent of Square and Cash App, popped after the company reported second-quarter results after the closing bell Thursday. However, the stock quickly pared those gains.

At first glance, there was a lot to love about Block's second-quarter print. Gross profit, a key performance indicator, rose 14% to $2.54 billion in the quarter, beating the $2.46 billion consensus estimate among analysts polled by FactSet.

The fintech saw growth across its business segments as well. Point-of-sale system Square logged gross profit of $1.03 billion, roughly in line with estimates and representing 11% growth year over year. Gross profit at Cash App, meanwhile, climbed 16% to $1.5 billion, topping the $1.44 billion Wall Street was looking for.

Block also boosted its full-year outlook. The company now expects $10.17 billion in gross profit, up from $9.96 billion. Management also guided for adjusted operating income of $2.03 billion, compared with a previous forecast for $1.9 billion.

While shares initially rose in after-hours trading Thursday following the report, they backtracked and fell 3% to $74.56 on Friday. It could be that Block's latest results, while encouraging, simply failed to meet lofty expectations.

Adjusted earnings of 62 cents a share narrowly missed the 63 cents analysts had projected, according to FactSet. More notably, net revenue fell 1.6% to $6.054 billion and came in below the $6.297 billion Wall Street was anticipating.

While it's possible that the stock simply rose too high before reversing course, the market reaction also follows a pattern. In the case of peers, less-than-perfect prints triggered steep declines. Shares of Fiserv and Toast, two point-of-sale providers, fell on the heels of their most recent quarterly earnings, with Fiserv cratering double-digits.

In Block's annual shareholder letter, CEO Jack Dorsey asserted that the company was "back on offense" after its latest quarter. "I'm confident in our ability to sustain strong growth at scale," the Twitter co-founder asserted.

Something else stood out — Dorsey's emphasis on cryptocurrency. "The next generation of consumers has a different relationship with money and wants the option to use bitcoin and other forms of payment as part of their financial lives," Dorsey wrote.

He noted that Cash App had recently increased withdrawal limits for Bitcoin, and that Square had showcased the ability for sellers to accept the cryptocurrency in May. "Our longstanding investment in bitcoin as a technology protocol represents a slow and steady conviction in a new open, decentralized paradigm for payments that doesn't rely on gatekeepers," Dorsey continued.

The attitude is reminiscent of another player in the fintech space. When Barron's spoke to SoFi Technologies CEO Anthony Noto last week, he, similarly, stressed SoFi's pivot to crypto, and laid out steps the company had taken to embrace Bitcoin and other digital currencies.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com

data.title: Brazil's Braskem, Unipar confirm talks after report of US plant sales
data.description:
Brazilian petrochemical companies Braskem (BRK53.SA) and Unipar said on Friday that they had kicked off talks regarding some of Braskem's assets.

Neither firm's stock exchange filing mentioned specific assets. A source with knowledge of the matter told Reuters that Unipar was weighing a deal to buy Braskem's units in Texas, Pennsylvania and West Virginia for around $1 billion, as first reported by newspaper O Globo.

Conversations have been ongoing for several weeks, but have not yet come close to a final agreement, the source said.

Both firms said that a confidentiality agreement had been signed, but that no other deal, binding or not, had been reached.

They declined to comment further when contacted by Reuters.

Braskem's chief executive officer had on Thursday denied any interest in selling the assets.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1144 ET - Coffee futures on the Intercontinental Exchange are up 2.8% to $3.06 a pound, catching support from an apparent decrease in coffee stored in ICE warehouses. "Some support is coming from the sharp decline in ICE certified stocks, which have fallen to their lowest level since May 2024 this week, dropping another 7,804 bags yesterday to 743,240," says ADM Investor Services in a note. These stocks are down as the 50% Brazilian tariff kicks in, which will impact the ability of U.S. roasters to import arabica from Brazil. If coffee finishes higher, then it'll be the fourth increase in the past five sessions. Futures have risen roughly 8% in that time. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1117 ET - Russian exports of oil and condensates have stayed relatively high at 4.3 million barrels a day in 1H25, down from 4.8 million b/d in 2024, although the destination in recent years has shifted more to Asia, the EIA says in an analysis as the oil market ponders the future of India's purchases. Europe's share of Russian exports fell to 11% in 1H25 from 51% in 2020, while Asia and Oceania's share rose to 81% last year from 41% in 2020. Exports from Russia to India increased from about 50,000 b/d in 2020 to 1.7 million b/d in 2024 and averaged 1.6 million b/d in 1H25, the EIA says. China remains the biggest buyer at 2 million b/d in 1H25. Oil futures are down for a seventh straight session in choppy trade, with WTI off 0.4% at $63.62 and Brent down 0.3% at $66.23. (anthon.harrup@wsj.com)

1110 ET - Gold prices continue to trade higher and are on track for weekly gains of nearly 3% after it emerged that the U.S. had imposed levies on imports of gold bars. "We're closely monitoring the situation to gauge its potential short and long-term impact on the cost of gold for investors, consumers and industry," says Joseph Cavatoni, strategist at the World Gold Council. "It remains to be seen how these tariffs could influence the broader gold market knowing the interlink between the OTC [over the counter] market in London and the futures contracts in the U.S." In evening trade in Europe, futures are up 1.2% to $3,493.70 a troy ounce after hitting a record high of $3,534.20 earlier. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1103 ET - After rising to over $2.32 a pound Thursday, live cattle futures on the CME are 1.7% lower, paring the October contract to roughly $2.28 a pound. Cattle have been higher on demand for beef ahead of the last major grilling holiday, Labor Day. Another factor supporting cattle this week is the implementation of 50% tariffs on imports from Brazil-which is where the U.S. gets a lot of its beef from. Buyers had been stocking on beef ahead of the tariffs coming in force, says ADM Investor Services. "Boxed beef values have been very strong this week as grinding beef coming in from Brazil is likely to end," says ADMIS in a note. Hogs are flat in morning trade. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1057 ET - Chinese data is showing that the country imported 11.7 million metric tons of soybeans in July, up 18% from this time last year. Overall, 61 million tons of soybeans have been imported in the first seven months of the year, up 4.6% from the same timeframe last year. While data for the origins of the soybeans is not yet available for July, in June 87% of the soybeans imported by China came from Brazil. With the current trade climate, U.S. sales may stay under wraps through the rest of the year, says Commerzbank in a note. "If the Chinese import tariffs on US soybeans remain at the current level of 15%, US exporters are likely to find it difficult to sell their soybeans to China." (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1036 ET - Wendy's cut its 2025 guidance for the second time this year as U.S. sales slump, but the stock isn't getting fried as the burger chain reports pockets of strength elsewhere. The company's profit ticked up to $55.1 million from $54.6 million and adjusted earnings of 29 cents a share topped analyst forecasts by 4 cents, according to FactSet. The company's operating profit was boosted by lower advertising spending, reorganization and realignment costs and overheads. Wendy's also highlighted that its international business delivered 8.7% higher systemwide sales during the quarter. Shares rise 1% to $10.05. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1026 ET - Grain traders are looking ahead to next week's WASDE report from the USDA. Yields and production is expected to rise from the previous month, with analysts debating how much they climb. Soybean yields may show the biggest uptick, says Karen Braun of Zaner Ag Hedge. "If I had to vote for a potential yield surprise scenario this year, it would be soybean yield to the high side," says Braun in a note. She points out that the USDA has raised its soybean yield forecast in its August report from the prior month in eight of the past 10 years, with the largest of those upticks being an increase of 7% in 2020. In morning trade, most-active corn is up 0.5%, soybeans are flat, and wheat is up 0.6%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0953 ET - Cobalt prices aren't likely to surge close to the highs recorded in 2022 even as the world's top producer, the Democratic Republic of Congo, extends the ban on exports, Oxford Economics Africa says. The ban, initially announced in February, will remain in place until September and is partly responsible for the 18% cobalt-production slump, to 41,720 metric tons in the first quarter, OEA notes. Congo accounts for over 70% of the world's cobalt production. "If the government reopens cobalt exports in 4Q 2025, it will likely introduce export quotas to prevent market flooding and subsequent price crashes," OEA says. "Ongoing production during the ban could also suggest a post-expiration supply surge, which will renew downward price pressure." (Nicholas.Bariyo@wsj.com;@Nicholasbariyo)

0908 ET - Oil futures pick up cautiously while on track for steep weekly losses after six straight sessions in the red. Much trading this week has focused on India's response to U.S. tariffs over its purchases of Russian oil, and to what extent India will cut back on those purchases. Plans for a Trump-Putin meeting have also taken off some of the upside. "Flat price and spreads are sending surprisingly bearish signals," Neil Crosby of Sparta Commodities says in a note. "It does seem like spreads should be reacting to evidence that various Indian refiners are fairly active on the spot crude market, both light and medium quality and from a variety of origins." WTI is up 0.4% at $64.16 and Brent is up 0.5% at $66.78.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0703 ET - The potential application of U.S. tariffs on imports of one-kilo gold bars would have a profound impact on the global bullion market, says Commerzbank Research's Carsten Fritsch. According to a Financial Times report, the U.S. Customs Border Protection agency said in a ruling letter that gold bars weighing one kilogram and 100 ounces should be classified under a code subject to levies. "If this is not a mistaken classification, it would have serious implications for the gold market," the commodity analyst says. "It would mean that gold bars of this size imported from Switzerland into the U.S. would be subject to a 39% tariff." In European afternoon trade, gold futures trade 0.6% higher at $3,475.40 a troy ounce after reaching a peak of $3,534.20 earlier in the session. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0654 ET - The economic impact of U.S. tariffs on Swiss goods is likely to be severe, the Association of Swiss Chocolate Manufacturers, or Chocosuisse, says in a statement. The country is subject to 39% duties in the U.S., a higher rate than initially set by President Trump. Swiss politicians must implement an effective package of measures to provide relief to affected companies and reinforce the country's long-term attractiveness as a business location, the trade organization says. For many member companies, the new tariff rate represents a serious challenge and, without mitigating measures, will jeopardize sales and jobs in the medium term, it says. A coordinated and united approach by the Federal Council and the business community is essential, the organization adds. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0639 ET - Palm oil ended slightly higher as traders awaited key data. Official palm oil supply and demand data from the Malaysian Palm Oil Board are due Monday. The country's July exports are expected to fall and put downward pressure on prices. However, the pressure could be limited, given declining exports from Indonesia as well, Ruida Futures analysts say in a research note. Indonesia's B40 mandate, which requires a 40% mix of palm oil in biodiesel, has left the country's palm oil exports in short supply, they add. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for October delivery ended 14 ringgit higher at 4,254 ringgit a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Over-the-counter shares of Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac jump after report Trump aiming for IPO of mortgage giants
data.description:
** Over-the-counter shares of Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac jump after a Wall Street Journal report that Trump administration is preparing to sell stock in mortgage giants later this year

** FNMA jumps 19.7% to $9.90, while FMCC up 19% at $7.89

** Plans under discussion could value the companies at roughly $500 billion or more combined, and fetch $30 billion - Wall Street Journal report

** Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac have been under federal conservatorship since 2008, following the financial crisis during which both entities became insolvent amid the subprime mortgage meltdown

** The companies did not immediately respond to Reuters' requests for comment

data.title: Fed nominee pushes on wobbly political firewalls
data.description:
Donald Trump named critic of independent monetary policy Stephen Miran to a seat at the central bank. Institutionalists once had veto power over norm-breaking nominees. Their waning clout portends consequences beyond near-term interest rates when it comes time for a new chair.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Gabriel Rubin on Bluesky and LinkedIn.

CONTEXT NEWS

U.S. President Donald Trump on August 7 said he would nominate Council of Economic Advisers Chairman Stephen Miran to replace Adriana Kugler, who is resigning from her seat as a governor at the U.S. Federal Reserve. Trump has also said that he has narrowed his possible choices to replace Chair Jerome Powell, whose term expires in May 2026.

Current Fed Governor Christopher Waller, along with White House economic adviser Kevin Hassett and former Fed Governor Kevin Warsh, have been floated by the administration as possible nominees for that position.

data.title: Is Gen X Nostalgia Just Trauma-Bonding? — WSJ
data.description:
By Adam Kirsch

As my 50th birthday approaches, something unsettling has started to happen: Social media has figured out that I'm a member of Generation X, and it wants me to feel nostalgic about it.

It's not that I mind opening Facebook and seeing photos of the cast of "Diff'rent Strokes" or quotes from "Seinfeld," though I try not to click on them — you have to be careful not to encourage the algorithm. What bothers me is the mythmaking about how tough we Xers had it growing up, and how tough we became as a result.

"One thing about Gen X is we ain't built soft. We got steel-toed souls," a man with a big graying beard declares in one video. We are "the original FAFO generation," another meme declares.

Apparently, everyone born between 1965 and 1980 spent their formative years doing things that would be regarded as insanely hazardous today: climbing metal playground equipment; riding in cars without seat belts; wandering in packs late at night. In one Facebook post, a fading photo shows a rec room decorated in 1970s browns and yellows, where a group of what looks like 10-year-olds are nonchalantly smoking cigarettes.

The implied contrast, of course, is with today's youth, who are coddled and helicoptered and have never even seen a pack of American Spirits. There's clearly an audience for this, or the memes wouldn't keep coming.

Older people have always liked to pat themselves on the back for being tougher than young people — like the father in the old joke who tells his kids that he walked 5 miles to school and back, uphill both ways. It's a way of compensating for decline: We may not look like much now, but you should have seen us when!

But there's a logical problem here, since the parents who raised today's soft, overprotected young people are us. If playgrounds are now required to have rubber mats under jungle gyms and teenagers don't leave home without their location trackers switched on, maybe it's a sign that, for all our bravado, we Gen Xers actually feel we could have used more protection, more supervision. Perhaps all those memes about Gen X toughness are a form of trauma-bonding.

The term "latchkey kid" became common in the 1970s and 1980s. Our childhood imaginations were shaped by stories of kidnappings. We all knew of Etan Patz, a 6-year-old boy who was abducted and murdered while walking to his school bus stop in Manhattan, and Adam Walsh, who was seven when he was taken and beheaded in Florida. TV stations used to broadcast a nightly public service announcement: "It's 10 p.m. Do you know where your children are?" The implication was that many parents didn't.

Things have certainly changed: last year in Georgia a mother got arrested for letting her 10-year-old walk to the store in broad daylight.

If I feel any tug of Gen X nostalgia, it doesn't have to do with surviving childhood perils. Perhaps that means I never felt underparented. Though I did have nightmares about Adam Walsh after the TV movie "Adam" came out in 1983 — how could I help it when we shared a name?

Rather, the Gen X archetype I feel some kinship with is the slacker, who dominated pop culture in the first half of the 1990s. If I made memes for Facebook groups like "The True Generation X!" (1.5 million members), I would take them from "Reality Bites" and "Kicking and Screaming," movies in which overeducated, underemployed 20-somethings take a perverse pride in their failure to launch. Or "Beavis and Butthead," the animated MTV show in which a pair of teenage cretins snicker through music videos. Or the video for Nirvana's "Smells Like Teen Spirit," in which Kurt Cobain moans, "I feel stupid and contagious."

This was the Gen X sensibility I remember: passive, mocking, skeptical. Our teenage rebellion took that form because we were in the tricky position of rebelling against a generation — our parents' — whose own teenage rebellion had been mythically glorious.

Growing up in the 1980s meant hearing constantly about the golden age of the 1960s — its politics, fashion, sexual liberation, mind-expanding drugs and above all its music. "Bliss was it in that dawn to be alive, /But to be young was very Heaven!" wrote the poet William Wordsworth about the days of the French Revolution. The youth of the 1960s never stopped feeling the same way.

Every generation needs to justify itself by showing why the previous one was wrong. Since it was impossible to outdo the baby boomers in either idealism or hedonism, Generation X zigzagged in the other direction, glorifying cynicism and apathy.

"We live small lives on the periphery; we are marginalized and there is much in which we choose not to participate," wrote Douglas Coupland in his 1991 novel "Generation X: Tales for an Accelerated Culture," which invented the label.

From the outside this could appear whiny. Young people in the '90s were duly derided as slackers, much as young people today are scorned as snowflakes. But this attitude was also what young people today would call "performative," designed for show. It was a shield for earnestness and vulnerability.

The writer who expressed this paradox best was David Foster Wallace, who became the literary voice of Generation X with his novel "Infinite Jest" in 1996. Wallace looked like he could be in a grunge band, and his book was a satire on American self-satisfaction. But he was also painfully sensitive, and understood the ways people use ironic detachment, entertainment and what he called "substances" to make life bearable.

"Drugs, movies where stuff blows up, loud parties — all these chase away loneliness," Wallace said in an interview when "Infinite Jest" was published. When he died by suicide in 2008, at the age of 46, it was a literary bookend to Kurt Cobain's suicide in 1994, one of Generation X's first milestone traumas.

Decades later, Xers still have a reputation for not getting involved. Another category of generational memes is about Gen X being caught between two much more vocal and confident generations, the Boomers and the Millennials: they yell at each other about politics while we sit off to the side looking bemused.

It seems fitting that there may never be a Gen X president. No one born between 1965 and 1980 has even been nominated. Kamala Harris, born in 1964, just missed the cutoff. Donald Trump and Joe Biden are our parents' age, while 2028 prospects such as JD Vance (born in 1984) and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (born in 1989) are classic Millennials, burning with ideological certainty.

As the America we grew up in disappears into the past, replaced by memes and myths, it's a good thing we have those steel-toed souls; I have a feeling we're going to need them.

data.title: US FDA says country's IV, injectable saline shortage is resolved
data.description:
A shortage of intravenous and injectable saline solutions in the United States has now been resolved, U.S. Food and Drug Administration commissioner Martin Makary said on Friday.

The shortage partly stems from Baxter International's North Carolina plant getting damaged in Hurricane Helene last year.

At that time, the Marion, North Carolina site was producing 60% of the nation's supply of IV fluids and peritoneal dialysis solutions, amounting to 1.5 million bags daily, according to the American Hospital Association.

A shortage of IV fluids, which provide nutrients to the body or replenish fluids lost due to injury or illness, had forced hospitals to curtail elective procedures.

Notes to Editors

GCP Infra is a closed-ended investment company and FTSE-250 constituent whose shares are traded on the main market of the London Stock Exchange. Its objective is to provide shareholders with regular, sustained, long-term distributions and to preserve capital over the long term by generating exposure to UK infrastructure debt and related and/or similar assets.

The Company primarily targets investments in infrastructure projects with long term, public sector-backed, availability-based revenues. Where possible, investments are structured to benefit from partial inflation protection. GCP Infra is advised by Gravis Capital Management Limited.

GCP Infra has been awarded with the London Stock Exchange's Green Economy Mark in recognition of its contribution to positive environmental outcomes.
